content: The Upanzi Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) Network has made remarkable progress in 2024, reinforcing its mission of driving secure and resilient digital transformation across Africa. Up to this year, the network has deployed five  Digital Public Goods (DPG) and Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) technologies that are pivotal in enhancing critical infrastructure. Notable achievements include the development of the prototype Upanzi Project Management System (UPMS) Social Safety Net Program, which facilitates digital identity verification for social benefits, and Mojashop, a platform demonstrating financial interoperability. Upanzi has also significantly contributed to public sector cybersecurity, testing over 260 financial apps and assessing 70,000 government e-services to strengthen digital cybersecurity safety and resilience. Furthermore, the network implemented Africa’s first academic Security Operations Centre (SOC) and hopes to provide hands-on training in security operations and help to build a new generation of cybersecurity professionals in Africa. Launching an open data portal, which organizes access to over 5,000 datasets, further illustrates the network’s commitment to data accessibility and capacity-building initiatives. Upanzi Network has expanded its efforts to engage and train students and professionals as part of the capacity-building initiatives. The picoCTF-Africa competition, for instance, drew over 3,500 participants, including 1,480 high school students, with a notable 23% female representation. The network also introduced externship programs, onboarding six external hosts and providing structured training to 15 students. This initiative is further complemented by the Digital Experience Center (DEC), a space designed to showcase emerging digital identity and financial technologies. This year, the center hosted 70 visitors, allowing them to explore the possibilities of digital infrastructure in promoting trust in government e-services. Upanzi's involvement in organizing three international conferences in Rwanda, Benin, and Sierra Leone also highlights its role as a facilitator of dialogue and collaboration among tech leaders and academics across Africa. The network has consistently demonstrated its commitment to fostering innovation and cultivating talent. Its Digital ID Hackathon engaged 178 students from five countries, promoting the development of practical solutions to digital identity challenges. Upanzi’s Professionals-in-Residence program has also been instrumental in building DPI expertise, enabling government officials and industry leaders to work on DPI projects at institutions like CMU-Africa. By offering immersive, hands-on learning experiences, Upanzi ensures that Africa’s digital infrastructure is secure, inclusive, and sustainable for future growth. As the network continues to scale its operations, it remains at the forefront of Africa's digital transformation, helping to bridge the gaps in digital identity management, financial inclusion, and cybersecurity. As part of our expansion effort in Africa, three members have been identified and are in the process of joining the Upanzi network in November 2024. This report provides a summary overview of the Upanzi Digital Public Infrastructure Network’s progress and activities for 2024, highlighting the strides made toward archiving the network’s strategic goals and thrust areas.

In an era when digital transformation has become mainstream in the political, social, economic, humanitarian, and development spheres, the Upanzi Digital Public Infrastructure Network stands at the forefront of advancing research, use, and deployment of digital public goods and infrastructure across Africa. Established to foster inclusive, secure, and resilient digital ecosystems, Upanzi builds and leverages existing openly available technologies and strategic partnerships to address critical use and deployment challenges in digital identity management, instant and interoperable payments, financial inclusion, data quality and accessibility, cybersecurity, internet connectivity, and resilience. Digital Public Goods and Infrastructure are essential for providing sustainable, inclusive development through open-source software, data, standards, and interconnectedness. Upanzi Network’s mission is to assess, research, deploy, prototype, and demonstrate DPG/DPIs to enhance governance and economic participation and alleviate poverty across the African continent. This report will encompass activities undertaken in deploying digital identity platforms, financial systems, open data portals, health data accessibility and portability, cybersecurity measures, internet connectivity and resilience, public health, and Technology with society projects.  Each project has been detailed in a specific section/thrust area, highlighting the progress made in 2024 and the impact made on the broader Digital Public Infrastructure landscape through various pathways that include but are not limited to continuous innovation and improvements, capacity building, and stakeholder engagement.
In this section we present the progress made in our different thrust areas over the last year.

subsections.title: DPGs and DPIs Governance and Deployment
subsections.overview: This year we have consolidated and expanded our activities in DPGs and DPIs, which involve DPG/DPI deployability, security assessment of DPGs and DPIs, use cases, as well as contribution to DPG/DPI implementation. In addition to the 5 DPG/DPI deployments over the first 2 years (MOSIP, Mojaloop, OpenCRVS, DHIS2), we have deployed MIFOS at the Upanzi Lab.

subsections.title: DPGs and DPIs Prototyping
subsections.overview: Our Digital ID and infrastructure teams successfully stabilized the MOSIP Digital ID instance and have now transitioned to maintenance mode and customizing ID cards to the local languages. The team also deployed and integrated Mifos into the Majaloop instance, interlinking the platform into one seamless mobile money, bank instant payment interoperable platform. The integration of Mifos makes seamless cross-border payments possible. A stable version of the Mojashop, a financial platform for merchants and customers, has also been deployed, as well as security and quality assurance testing. These efforts demonstrate and strengthen the foundation for digital ID and financial transactions as critical components for increased economic participation and service delivery. The team is currently engaging with the Majaloop Foundation and are planning to host a Majaloop Cybersecurity Bootcamp on the 11th November 2024, and the team is looking to provide a high-level overview of Majaloop and the process of deploying to key stakeholders. The team is also looking to conduct load/stress testing on the Majaloop instance to improve its reliability and security performance in collaboration with Majaloop Foundation.

subsections.title: Cybersecurity: Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing
subsections.overview: In 2024, our Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing (VAPT) team created and deployed a web-based platform integrated with security tools that assess secret scripts within mobile applications. Through this platform, developers can upload their apps, from which they receive vulnerability reports and recommendations that are automatically generated using AI and machine learning algorithms. The team also increased the platform's performance and output this year, making the resulting output faster and more readable for the user. As part of Upanzi Network’s cybersecurity awareness initiatives this year, the team assessed over 70,000 government e-services across 53 African countries and identified 400 vulnerabilities, including 120 severe incidences. The cybersecurity team is contacting the identified stakeholders to highlight the issues and provide recommendations. The team has started engagements at the country level, starting with Rwanda, Kenya, Ethiopia, and Senegal.

subsections.title: Cybersecurity: Academic Security Operation Centres
subsections.overview: Upanzi successfully established and tested a robust Security Operations Center (SOC) within its lab environment, now transitioning to real-world testing. Student internships and practicum projects were provided, focusing on integrating digital forensics, while a cybersecurity course was launched at CMU-Africa to equip students with SOC skills. Continuous improvements enhanced the SOC's flexibility, with features of open-source tools documented for future use. A proposal was submitted to CMU-Africa’s Center for Inclusive Digital Transformation of Africa (CIDTA) to establish cybersecurity labs in partner universities.

subsections.title: Cybersecurity: Smishing
subsections.overview: The affordable SMS data collection infrastructure to combat Mobile Money scams in Africa u,” A Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Give-Directly, is currently being reviewed by both parties, with plans in place to develop and host a dedicated data collection portal for the organization. At the same time, Upanzi is actively deploying honeynet infrastructure at Université Cheikh Anta Diop de Dakar (UCAD) to enhance cybersecurity efforts. Public access to the project's GitHub repository has been granted, providing open access to the honeynet infrastructure for broader collaboration and development. Additionally, the project was successfully showcased at the ACM Compass 2024 conference, demonstrating its impact and technical capabilities.

subsections.title: Cybersecurity: Africa Cybersecurity Lab Expansion
subsections.overview: In 2024, the Upanzi Network has engaged multiple universities across the continent, including: The University of Gaston Berger in Senegal, as part of the broader plan to set up and expand cybersecurity labs in Africa, the team engaged with the School of Information Science and CIRST University group and plans to assist the university in setting up and running an academic Security Operations Centre. The University of Dakar in Senegal is part of the Afretech network. Upanzi Network engaged with the University of Senegal with plans to also set up a Cybersecurity Lab. The first component is installing a Security Operations Centre and enrolling some of their students in the cybersecurity course recently developed and approved by CMU-Africa. Upanzi Network also engaged the Université Cheikh Anta Diop de Dakar, which is looking to expand and scale up research and cybersecurity related to smishing. The smishing system has been deployed at the University of Université Cheikh Anta Diop de Dakar. A similar engagement and platform will also be shipped to the Give Directly Office in Rwanda to increase awareness of smishing among its clients.

subsections.title: Data: OpenData Portal and Health Data Portability
subsections.overview: The OpenData Portal has achieved significant milestones this year, including establishing an admin backend portal and collecting over 5,000 datasets aligned with the SDGs. Efforts are underway to link Upanzi Network’s data quality framework and modules into the portal, thus providing an integrated open data quality assessment platform. The Health Data Portability project developed a complete proof of concept web variant for medical records sharing, advancing towards seamless patient portability and improving healthcare outcomes. The team also engaged with critical stakeholders like Africa CDC and heads of public health at the Health Information Exchange (HIE) Maturity Assessment Tool Validation Workshop forum in Ghana in August 2024, and critical feedback was provided. The team also plans to engage research and solution architects from the Makerere University School of Public Health in Uganda in the coming months to advance this prototype to key health institutions like Mulago Hospital.

subsections.title: Connectivity: Internet Connectivity and Resilience
subsections.overview: Measurement of Internet Resilience Africa Project (MIRA) in collaboration with Internet Society: The MIRA infrastructure was successfully deployed at the Upanzi Lab, with plans to send MIRA pods to at least three countries to expand broadband measurement efforts. The project ventured into broadband measurement, with the design of the measurement infrastructure completed. Phase I of the broadband measurement was conducted in Kigali, marking a significant step forward. To contribute to knowledge sharing, a research article was submitted to IMC, accompanied by a poster titled 'Towards A Low-Cost Mobile Internet Measurement Infrastructure: An Initial Deployment in Africa.' This initiative aims to enhance internet resilience across the continent.

subsections.title: Connectivity: Rural Connectivity Project
subsections.overview: The team made modifications and installations of Bluetooth BLE, LoRa, and message generator scripts and installed and configured buzzer devices for small-scale testing. The GSM/GPS module was successfully integrated into the mesh network, and the Edge computing paradigm was established by connecting various communication technologies with the storage module. Proof-of-concept tests were conducted within the lab, addressing severalany issues identified. Collaborative meetings have been held, leading to the development an MoU with Dronlytics to deploy project sections further. The team is finalizing a research paper (80% complete) for submission to the 10th ICICT 2025 Hybrid Conference in London. The team plans to collaboratively deploy this project in the refugee environment in partnership with World Vision, looking to connect schools and communities. The team has visited the refugee camp and had discussions with the Humanitarian Emergency and Education Emergency teams where a promising application of the system in  sanitation and hygiene information-sharing  across different camp zones through the WASH committee structure, as well as a platform for sharing educational content was proposed. The Upanzi Network technical team is now working on scaling and adopting the system to the highlighted needs and are hoping to deploy early next year.

subsections.section: Public Health, Agriculture, and Climate Change: Malaria Detection and  Digitisation
subsections.overview: This year, the team implemented the  Data Access model for patient management. It was tested using mock data, and a testing and digitization lab was set up at CyLab-Africa to facilitate the project. A micro-geographic analysis of malaria vector spread is currently being conducted using Kamabuye Health Center as a case study, with 21,000 data points collected for analysis. The segmentation and classification algorithms were optimized using new metrics commonly applied in medical imaging. A basic public dashboard has been developed and is now undergoing vulnerability assessment, penetration testing, and quality assurance tests, displaying key indicators. The integration pipeline is currently being tested with mock data to ensure functionality.

subsections.title: Public Health, Agriculture, and Climate Change: Humeka FL
subsections.overview: The Humeka FL project focuses on the early detection of Birth Asphyxia (BA), a leading cause of neonatal mortality, especially in sub-Saharan Africa. Traditional diagnostic methods, such as APGAR scores and advanced technologies like MRI, are either prone to errors or inaccessible in low-resource settings. HumekaFL utilizes Federated Learning (FL) to train machine learning models on newborn cry data across healthcare organizations while maintaining data privacy by decentralizing the process. The project’s Federated SVM model outperformed existing solutions, achieving a 95.88% accuracy in detecting BA, surpassing the Ubenwa benchmark. This year, the team focused on refining the model to improve its accuracy and strengthen its privacy aspects by working with the Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing team and making it more reliable in low-resource environments. The team is also currently looking to deploy the application at more health facilities. The project was accepted for publication and was presented at the 2024 COMPASS conference in July 2024.

subsections.title: Public Health, Agriculture, and Climate Change: Generating a water efficiency dataset for electricity generation and cooling towers in Rwanda
subsections.overview: As AI technologies continue to advance, the demand for data centers is rapidly increasing. These data centers require significant amounts of energy and water for cooling, posing challenges in water-scarce regions such as Africa. This project seeks to  address these challenges by developing a comprehensive 5 dataset to guide water management and data center placement decisions in Africa, with an initial focus on Rwanda. This year, the team collected weather and energy mix data for several African countries, focusing on developing a methodology tailored to the African context. Datasets from 11 African countries, including Rwanda, were gathered and supplemented by global data for comparative analysis. A methodology adapted from studies on water usage efficiency in data centers was developed for Africa. This led to creation of a comprehensive water efficiency dataset, categorizing countries by climate type and assessing their water consumption. The dataset was further applied to evaluate water consumption associated with various AI models, including OpenAI's GPT series. The initial results of this project have been accepted for publication and will be presented at the 2024 NeurIPS Workshops in December 10-15 in Vancouver, Canada.

subsections.title: Technology and Society: Responsible Research and Innovation
subsections.overview: In 2024, Upanzi Network played a pivotal role in advancing Responsible Research and Innovation (RRI) as part of the broader initiative at Carnegie Mellon University -Africa. This project focused on promoting responsible and ethical practices in digital transformation and research to ensure that technical advancements are aligned with social values and needs. The Upanzi team’s contributions focused on integrating ethical frameworks into DPGs and DPI projects, emphasizing transparency, inclusivity, and sustainability. Their work has set a benchmark for responsible digital innovation across the African continent, contributing to the global conversation on ethical technology deployment.

subsections.title: Capacity building: PicoCTF
subsections.overview: Through the picoCTF program, close to 1500 participants were engaged across 15 African countries—the most significant number of participants the competition has had to date, with female participation increasing nearly 30%. The picoCTF team has developed 35 new challenges that participants would undertake and is currently engaged with the Ministry of ICT and Ministry of Education in Rwanda to set up the first high school teacher training sessions in 5 schools. These sessions are geared towards having more teachers acquire the knowledge and skills to inspire and encourage student participation and guidance. The picoCTF team has also developed the post-program impact assessment for 2022-2024.

subsections.title: Capacity building: Digital ID Hackathon
subsections.overview: The inaugural Regional East African Digital ID Hackathon, in collaboration with MSC consulting and MOSIP, saw over 170 registrations in 5 East African countries, resulting in 18 submissions and ongoing support for the prototyping. This initiative has allowed the team to learn, train, capacitate, and create awareness among over 100 students about Digital ID skills and knowledge. The team plans to roll out the West, North, and Southern Africa Regional Hackathons in November 2024, closely working with the newly signed Upanzi Network Nodes in Morocco, South Africa, and Botswana targeting 1000 registrations and 120 idea submissions. Winning teams from all African regions will be invited to present their prototype during the ID4Africa Annual General Meeting (AGM) to be held in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia in May 2025. The team is also actively engaging more strategic partners to come and support the Hackathon; these include Okta for Good, Nethope, and Credebel. The team has also joined International Identity Day as an NGO partner to commemorate, advocate, and underscore the fundamental importance of having verifiable proof of identity.

subsections.title: Capacity building: Professional in Residence Program
subsections.overview: The Professional in Residence Program was launched in 2024 and started with the Upanzi Network's collaboration with the Ethiopian National ID program. The first cohort sees three Ethiopian National ID program professionals come to the Upanzi Network/Cylab Africa premises for six months to work on improving biometric enrollment systems and fraud detection mechanisms.

subsections.title: Capacity building: Internship Program
subsections.overview: The summer internship program 2024 saw 40 students join Upanzi Network/Cylab-Africa for three months to work and contribute to the existing digital research, technology and infrastructure projects, and cybersecurity. Students expanded their knowledge and scaled up the projects. The students also presented the project to both internal and external stakeholders. Some of the summer projects are listed below:        Autism Feeding Application The Autism Feeding Project aims to address feeding challenges faced by children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) through a mobile application designed to support parents in managing these difficulties at home. Focusing on the African context, particularly South Africa, where limited research exists, the project used a mixed-methods approach to gather data from therapists, caregivers, and parents. A low-fidelity prototype was developed and refined through user testing, with input from health professionals and caregivers. The final goal is to create a fully functional app that improves feeding behavior and nutrition in children with ASD, alongside publishing a research paper that highlights the project's findings and its effectiveness compared to existing solutions.       Child Online Safety - supervised by Professor Edith Luhanga  The Child Online Safety research project, conducted in Rwanda and Tanzania, focused on co-designing online safety applications with children and parents to address risks from increased internet usage. Through surveys and interviews with over 120 participants, the project identified key needs for online safety tools, aiming to create web and mobile applications that balance parental control with children's autonomy. Initial findings showed the effectiveness of co-designing, with feedback supporting a collaborative approach to managing online risks like harmful content and cyberbullying. Further testing and refinement of the applications are planned to enhance online safety for children.          Maternal Health Chatbots - supervised by Professor Edith Luhanga  The Maternal Health Chatbots project, led by Prof. Edith Luhanga with interns Floris Nzabakira and Daniel Owolabi, aimed to reduce maternal mortality by simplifying and translating complex maternal health information into low-resource languages using Large Language Models (LLMs). Through literature reviews, data collection, and mini experiments, the team tested LLMs' ability to improve the accessibility of health information. Significant progress was made in text simplification and translation while maintaining meaning. Future plans include expanding language coverage, refining user profiling, and deploying the chatbots to improve maternal health outcomes in diverse linguistic and literacy contexts.          Addressing Biases in Neurological and Respiratory Monitoring Technologies in African Populations  This project, led by CMU-Africa in collaboration with CMU Pittsburgh, aims to address biases in neurological and respiratory monitoring technologies for African populations. It focuses on improving diagnostic tools like EEG and SpO2, which face challenges due to phenotypic differences, such as thick hair impacting EEG contact and melanated skin affecting SpO2 accuracy. The team developed the Sevo clip to secure EEG electrodes in braided hair and began refining methodologies for SpO2 measurements. Despite some delays, data collection protocols and training sessions were established. Future work includes large-scale testing and expanded partnerships to enhance patient care in sub-Saharan Africa.        Enhancing Cybersecurity Collaboration in East Africa: Challenges, Opportunities and Recommendations - supervised by Professor Jema Ndibwile  This project examined cybersecurity cooperation across East African Community (EAC) member countries, focusing on public-private partnerships, regional collaboration, and incident response mechanisms. It identified challenges such as disparities in cybersecurity frameworks and the slow ratification of the African Union's Malabo Convention, which hinder regional strategies. Kenya and Rwanda were noted for their advances in cybersecurity infrastructure, with potential for regional knowledge sharing. The research emphasized the need for harmonized policies, capacity building, and international support, providing recommendations to strengthen cooperation and enhance the region’s resilience to cyber threats, critical for digital economic growth.       Cybersecurity and Online Safety Awareness through Gamified Interactive Digital Storytelling (GIDS) - supervised by Professor Jema Ndibwile  This project aims to enhance cybersecurity awareness among young people in vulnerable environments, such as refugee camps, using EdTech and STEM solutions. The team developed the GIDS framework, which incorporates interactive digital storytelling and gamification to teach key cybersecurity concepts like privacy, cyberbullying, phishing, and malware. With the framework 90% complete, the next phase involves creating stories and testing the platform in a low-resource setting, such as the Mahama Refugee Camp. Future efforts will focus on assessing the platform’s impact, ensuring scalability, sustainability, and refining it based on user feedback.

subsections.title: Capacity building: Externship Program
subsections.overview: The externship program 2024 saw six students and Research assistants work with the Network’s DPG/DPI partners and implementers, including MOSIP and Majaloop. An additional 15 students were also given introductory training sessions on MOSIP, Mojaloop, and OpenCRVs DPG/DPI. The externship program has students and research assistants paired up with external DPG/DPI partners to tackle a particular aspect of the useability or deployability of the DPG/DPI.

subsections.title: Capacity building: Smartphone Penetration for Guiding Digitization Strategies Project Case study, Rwanda
subsections.overview: A summer project under the supervision of Prof Eric Umohoza and the Upanzi Network, in collaboration with RURA ( Rwanda Utilities Regulatory Authority), successfully analyzed smartphone penetration and delivered a comprehensive framework for guiding digitization strategies in Rwanda. The project established a robust data pipeline for collecting and integrating smartphone penetration and usage data, developed a detailed data cleaning and preprocessing framework, and conducted exploratory data analysis to identify key trends and correlations. A segmentation model was built to distinguish consumer segments, and the team proposed a predictive model to be developed to forecast future smartphone penetration rates and market saturation levels. The findings were presented through an interactive dashboard, and actionable recommendations were made to guide digital transformation efforts in the ICT sector. The project culminated in delivering comprehensive documentation and a detailed research report, ensuring knowledge transfer to relevant stakeholders.

subsections.title: Capacity building: Africa Threat Intelligence Project
subsections.overview: The Africa Threat Intelligence Project, in collaboration with AfricaCERT, focused on deploying honeypots for threat intelligence in Africa. The team was able to successfully build a robust mechanism to collect threat intelligence data from the designed honeynets and continuously aggregate the data  to a centralized node. The team also developed a customizable dashboard for different stakeholders to view threat intelligence.  In collaboration with AfricaCERT, the honeynet infrastructure has already been deployed in 2 countries. As part of a broad deployment effort, the team is holding a training session about the infrastructure during the 2024 FIRST & AfricaCERT Symposium to be held in Livingstone, Zambia on November 26-28. Participants to the training session are from national CERTs from various countries in Africa.

subsections.title: Upanzi Network Nodes
subsections.overview: 2024 saw the first call for applications for university-affiliated technology labs to apply to be part of the Upanzi Digital Public Infrastructure Network. The team received nine applications from universities across the continent. After careful consideration, three universities, Al-Alkhawayn University from Morocco, the University of Botswana from Botswana, and Wits University from South Africa, were chosen as nodes of the Upanzi Network. The team plans to make the announcement at the Morocco Cybersecurity Symposium on November 21st and 22nd of November, 2024. During the application process, the nodes were requested to develop digital transformation projects they would implement during their tenure as nodes. Al Alkhawayn University plans to focus on improving cybersecurity collaboration in the country and the region. University of Botswana wants to expand its AI and remote sensing research to improve livestock management. Wits University is looking to enhance linguistic learning through virtual reality gaming applications. Upanzi Network has also engaged Addis Ababa University of Science and Technology, University of Nairobi, University of Lagos, and the University of Rwanda to join the Upanzi Network. All Universities could submit applications to join the network; however, the applications needed to meet the requirements. The universities were given feedback and encouraged to apply to the next cohort of applications.The team plans to increase the number of nodes to five in 2025.

subsections.title: Knowledge Transfer: Government Advisory Unit
subsections.overview: In 2024, Upanzi Network launched the Government Advisory Unit, designed to offer neutral expert guidance on digital research, DPG/DPI tools, platforms, software, reliability, deployability, data management, and broader digital transformation initiatives. The unit aims to support governments leveraging digital technologies for inclusive and sustainable development. The team has made significant strides, engaging with Ethiopia’s National ID program to enhance their digital identity efforts. The Upanzi Network is  now actively looking to collaborate with the National ICT Authority of Kenya to drive further digital transformation initiatives. This advisory service positions Upanzi as a critical partner for African governments, supporting their digital journeys with innovative, research-backed solutions.

subsections.title: Knowledge Transfer: Digital Experience Centre
subsections.overview: The Digital Experience Center (DEC) established by the Upanzi Network at Carnegie Mellon University Africa is a physical space designed to showcase cutting-edge digital technologies, with an emphasis on digital public infrastructure (DPI) and digital public goods (DPG). The DEC immerses visitors in simulated environments, such as the 'Upanzi Republic,' where they interact with solutions like digital identity, payments, and cybersecurity. The center aims to foster collaboration between academia, government, and industry while driving research, capacity building, and resource optimization for sustainable development and The Upanzi Network team also attended the East African Digital Leaders Forum, where the Network’s projects, goals, and achievements were shared with digital champions from all East African countries and development partners. The digital leaders were also provided with the first Digital Experience Centre (DEC) tour, which sparked more interest and advocacy for DPIs within the respective delegates’ countries. The team looks forward to officially launching the Digital Experience Centre early next year. The Upanzi Network is targeting to have at least ten strategic partner tours and demonstrations before the close-up of the year.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: 50 in 5 campaign
subsections.overview: The Upanzi Network recently joined the 50 in 5 campaign, where the team is contributing to sharing insights, research, knowledge, and experiences on supporting governments and organizations in deploying and effectively using DPGs/DPIs.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: Digital for Development Hub
subsections.overview: The Network has also applied to be part of the Digital for Development (D4D) hub,  a strategic platform that aims to strengthen digital cooperation between the European Union and its Member States (Team Europe) and partners in Africa, Asia-Pacific, Latin America and the Caribbean, and the EU neighboring countries. The team plans to be part of their civil society and academia advisory group, providing knowledge, research, and experience on how the network is advancing the digital transformation agenda across Africa.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: Community of Practice: DPI4D ( Digital Public Infrastructure for Development)
subsections.overview: The Network has also been invited to join the  the Digital Public Infrastructure for Development community of practice: A Global Collaboration on Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI), which aims to ensure long-term gains from the rapid deployment of DPIs and continuous investment in building research-driven policy insights and cross-border learning. DPI4D is designed to address this need by bringing together a global network of leading think tanks to exchange ideas, inform policy discussions, and collaborate on research.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: MOSIP Connect 2024
subsections.overview: The Upanzi team attended the first-ever MOSIP global event, MOSIP Connect, in March 2024. The Upanzi Network team actively participated in the MOSIP Connect event, showcasing their innovative work in prototyping and deploying MOSIP for various use cases. Attended by over 300 global delegates, the event highlighted Upanzi's contributions to customizing MOSIP for Digital ID solutions, integrating it into their ecosystem to enhance secure identity verification, and showcasing how digital ID can improve access to social services. The team demonstrated real-world applications such as authenticating beneficiaries in social programs and customizing the platform for diverse identity needs across Africa, underscoring their commitment to scalable, open-source solutions for digital identity management. This participation further solidified Upanzi's role in fostering digital public infrastructure and collaboration across the global identity community.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: ID4Africa 2024
subsections.overview: In May 2024, the Upanzi Network team attended the 10th edition of the ID4Africa event in South Africa. Recognized as one of the largest and most influential gatherings focused on Digital identity management. The event's theme focused on  Digital Identity as DPI for fostering trust, inclusion, and adoption. The team was able to talk about, showcase, and demonstrate the work on prototyping and deploying Digital ID systems like MOSIP and engage new possible collaborations. At this event, the team ignited the need for a Digital ID Hackathon to spur more Africa-centric use cases for different stakeholders.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: Nethope
subsections.overview: The Network has also explored collaborations with Nethope, a global consortium of over 60 humanitarian and development not-for-profit organizations that are focused on advancing the digital transformation agenda across Africa and the globe. The next hopes to share its knowledge, expertise, training, and capacitate member organizations and collaborate with expanding and integrating Upanzi Network projects into solving their operational challenges.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: DIAL
subsections.overview: Upanzi Network has also explored areas of collaboration with Digital Impact Alliance (DIAL) in regions that provide expert knowledge and technical guidance in developing trustworthy data exchange platforms and consent mechanisms in Africa. The team is also currently exploring ways to be part of DIAL and  Africa CDC’s Health Technology Market place program, through which the team would be able to share and demonstrate the digital Identity health projects that have been developed through the network and also share knowledge and experience to other key stakeholders so that the network is at the frontlines of the digital ID health transformation f be part of the digital health transformation of Africa.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: Geneva Digital Health Hub
subsections.overview: In 2024, the Upanzi Network joined the Community of Practice on the Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) hosted by the Geneva Digital Health Hub in collaboration with the Swiss Institute for Tropical and Public Health (Swiss TPH) and Médecins Sans Frontières / Doctors without Borders(MSF). A Forum where the network would like to expand its network, acquire more partnerships, and showcase the public health projects the lab has been working on as part of its push to get more projects tested and expand out in the real world. These projects included the Humeka FL project, the Malaria Digitalisation Diagnosis Project, and the Health Portability project.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: University of Grenoble Alpes
subsections.overview: Upanzi Network has also engaged with the University of Grenoble Alpes through workshop sessions, during which each team presented the research and cybersecurity projects under their portfolio. The goal is to find relevant and overlapping projects that allow the exchange of synergies, ideas, knowledge, and research collaborations.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: DPI Summit
subsections.overview: The Upanzi Network actively participated in the Global Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) Summit held in Cairo from October 1-3, 2024. As part of the summit, the Upanzi team contributed to discussions on  'Resource Labs: Making Digital Public Infrastructure Work for Different Contexts' to drive sustainable development across Africa. Upanzi Network’s presence was particularly noted in panels and experience-sharing sessions about scaling digital identity systems, improving cybersecurity measures, and fostering inclusive innovation. The summit brought together leaders from over 100 countries, and Upanzi Network's efforts were highlighted as a significant contributor to Africa's digital transformation journey. The team plans to continue to shape the future of DPI, aligning with global efforts to accelerate digital access to essential services such as healthcare, education, and financial inclusion.

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: Upanzi Network Webinars
subsections.overview: The 2024 Upanzi Webinar Series has effectively brought together leading experts and practitioners to address some of the most pressing issues in digital public goods ( DPG) and digital transformation. From digital health innovations to cross-border identification, data privacy, and cybersecurity, each webinar has provided insights into both the technical and socio-technical aspects crucial to building a sustainable digital ecosystem in Africa. Looking forward, the Upanzi Network is committed to expanding the reach and impact of our webinar series in 2025. We plan to engage a broader audience through enhanced communication tools, including targeted social media campaigns and strategic partnerships. By strengthening our outreach, we aim to boost participation and ensure that these valuable discussions and learning opportunities are accessible to more individuals, driving forward a skilled DPG workforce and fostering greater public engagement in secure, fair, and inclusive digital development across the continent. As shown below, we have held five webinars this year, with two more scheduled before the end of 2024.        January 31, 2024 Topic: 'Digital Transformation of Health Campaigns' Speakers: Jojo Mehra, eGov Foundation;     February 28, 2024 Topic: 'Community-driven Massive Online Open Validation and Evaluation for Continuous Real-World Alignment in Medical Large Language Models' Speakers: Prof. Annie Hartley, Yale University;      March 27, 2024 Topic: 'Cross border Identification in Africa: Challenges and Opportunities' Speaker: Andrew Musoke, CMU-Africa;     June 4, 2024 Topic: 'Secure and Private Data Sharing' Speaker: Prof. Carsten Maple, The Alan Turing Institute;      October 25, 2024 Topic: 'Timing Attacks on Constant-Time Code' Speaker: Prof. Riccardo Paccagnella, CyLab, CMU

subsections.title: Stakeholder Engagement: Upanzi Digital Public Infrastructure Fellowship
subsections.overview: Last year, the Upanzi Network provided a fellowship to 4 students to allow them to attend courses at CMU-Africa and participate in the Upanzi Network’s impactful projects. Building on that success, the fellowship program continues to grow, with three new fellows from Ethiopia and Nigeria joining this year as cohort 2. These new fellows are excited to join and are particularly passionate about cybersecurity capacitation initiatives, tech entrepreneurship, and data analysis. Their involvement in the network’s project will contribute to the Network’s mission of driving digital public infrastructure transformation while fostering innovation and capacity building across Africa.       Quotes from Upanzi Fellow Cohort 1: Martha Kachweka - As a CyLab Africa scholar studying for my master's in data science at Carnegie Mellon University, I am passionate about using AI and machine learning to address urgent health issues in Africa. I envision creating innovative solutions that not only enhance healthcare outcomes but also prioritize the privacy and security of data. In today’s fast-paced world of AI, I am dedicated to developing responsible and secure data-driven systems that can genuinely transform healthcare delivery while safeguarding sensitive information. With the support of Upanzi’s network, I hope to contribute to building a healthier and more digitally secure future for Africa.;    Samuel Johnny - Grant Sanderson once said, 'Success is a function of the value you bring to others,' and the Upanzi Network embodies this philosophy completely. I'd add that 'Maximizing the value given to you is a function of how it's presented.' Joining the Upanzi Network as a scholar has been instrumental in fulfilling my lifelong dream of becoming a researcher. This community of brilliant minds has directly contributed to developing my research skills.  One memorable moment was a pivotal conversation with Assane, the co-director of Upanzi. As an enthusiastic fellow passionate about improving African digital health systems, I shared my interests with him. Assane, with his extensive vision for Upanzi, provided invaluable guidance tailored to my aspirations. What impressed me most was Upanzi's approach to Digital Public Goods (DPG) and their application to governance. The team's dedication to developing open-source digital solutions and applying them to improve governance structures in Africa was truly inspiring. This showcased Upanzi's commitment to creating sustainable, accessible digital infrastructure that can transform public services across the continent. While my focus remained on health systems, observing Upanzi's work in DPG governance gave me valuable insights into how digital solutions can be leveraged to enhance transparency, efficiency, and accessibility in various sectors.;    Kafui Akpalu - Working with the Upanzi network has enhanced my cybersecurity skills, scalable application development, and design thinking expertise. At Upanzi, I am treated not as a student but as a colleague, with the exact high expectations for quality and rigor. This perspective has instilled in me a commitment to delivering work of the highest standard, grounded in first-principles thinking. The experience has been invaluable to my academic and professional growth.

subsections.title: Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion: Upanzi Team
subsections.overview: The Upanzi team has grown to over 29 personnel, with 8  of the team members being female. The team continues to work with students, with over 10 students working as research assistants, 5 of whom are female.

subsections.title: Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion: Project team compositions
subsections.overview: As part of the diversity and inclusion initiative, the Upanzi team is intentionally encouraging gender diversity within each project striving  to balance gender with at least one female team member required on each project. This will advert male-only and female-only project team compositions.

subsections.title: Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion: Underserved  communities
subsections.overview: In collaboration with the CMU-Africa Diversity and Inclusion Office, Upanzi team has continued to work closely with World Vision and other refugee education implementing partners to provide training to ICT teachers within Mahama and Kiziba camp. The team has also been exploring opportunities to work with Save the Children’s Kumwe hub to increase access to and use of digital tools and technologies for schools and businesses.

subsections.title: Monitoring and Evaluation
subsections.overview: Number of implemented and deployed use cases - The team continued the development of MOSIP digital ID, the heath data portability, Upanzi Project Management System (UPMS) - The team had a couple of server instabilities with the MOSIP instance. This is something they highlighted to the MOSIP team that has been rectified and improved in the new installation process of the platform;      Number of open-source solutions deployed - OpenCRVS (1), Majaloop (1), Mifos (1) - The team plans to continue working closely with the Mifos and Majaloop teams to improve their system integrations and develop and deploy more use cases.;        Number of demonstrations to government officials by CMU-Africa and by other institutions in the network is 4 - A tour of the Digital Experience Centre by the East African Digital Leaders forum on DPI 13th-14th of August 2024. A delegation of 30 personnel was given a guided tour of the digital experience center and the projects implemented by Upanzi Network. CIDTA Summit on African Enterprenuership Ecoystem 28th August 2024 A delegation of 30 entrepreneurs was given a guided tour of the Digital Experience Centre and the projects and digital tools used in the Upanzi Network. Rose Hulman Institute of Technology- September 2024 Three delegates from the Rose Hulman Institute of technology were provided with an overview of the Upanzi Network and a guided tour of the DEC;     Number of participants in the various convenings - MOSIP Connect 2024: 24 participants, East African Digital Leaders forum on DPI: 3 participants, ID4Africa 2024: 2 participants, DPI Summit: 1 participant - The team is making an effort to have more presence and representation at convenings and conferences in 2025.;        Number of followers in social media platforms - Twitter: 513 Followers, LinkedIn: 836 Followers, Instagram: 266 Followers - Developing a strategy with the communication and marketing team at CMU-Africa to amplify our social media reach. Going forward, the team is also looking to focus more on LinkedIn.;        Number of demos performed in open-house and number of participants is 8 - Setting up a roaster a digital form that will allow for a more structured in house roaster for demonstrations. This will give more opportunity and time for all projects to present and demo their work.;     Number of trainings conducted and number of participants - PicoCTF 12 trainings, 120 participants, Digital ID Hackathon- 8 trainings, 178 participants, ICT Teachers training- 2 pieces of training, 60 participants - The team is looking to scale up these numbers in 2025, with the ID Hackathon set to launch in North, South and West Africa.;     Number of skills-building challenges deployed and number of participants - picoCTF-Africa 2024, Participants: 1436, Challenges developed: 35, Female participation: +110%, Number of students certified: 85 - The team plans to increase these numbers across more countries including Rwanda, Tanzania, Uganda, Kenya, Ethiopia, Botswana, Zimbabwe,Sierra Leone ,Malawi,Kenya,Uganda, Ghana, Morocco, Ethiopia,Nigeria, Senegal, Tunisia.;        Number of outreach engagements conducted - 6 outreach engagements: The team carried out university outreach engagements in Rwanda and Kenya, visiting faculty and students to increase awareness of picoCTF across multiple universities. - The team plans to scale up these engagements bring more awareness about the picoCTF;        Number of participants trained to use the developed tools by gender and by location - 17 students trained on how to use the MOSIP platform to develop Digital ID use cases, 13 male and 4 female  from Uganda, Rwanda and Ethiopia - The inaugural East African Digital ID Hackathon saw close to 170 students apply for the event. In year 4 the team plans to have a larger number of participants from countries in North, East and Southern Africa regions.;        Number of African researchers collaborating in the network by gender - 6 male and 6 female - Through the Upanzi Network expansion of nodes in Morocco, the team plans to work with Dr Houda, in Botswana, the team plans to work closely with Professor Shiek and at Wits University, the team plans work closely with Professor Leketi Makalela.;      Number of conference and journal publications - 6 - The team attended the ID4Africa, MOSIP connect and the DPI Summit. Our work on Smishing, RRI and cybesecurity have been published. The team plans increase the number of publications next with at least 5 more publications on the mature projects.

subsections.title: Publications and Press Releases
subsections.overview: A honeynet infrastructure to battle SMS scammers; Responsible research and innovation in Sub-Saharan Africa; Trustworthy artificial intelligence for the greater good; The Upanzi Network and MicroSave Consulting (MSC) announce Digital ID Hackathon Africa; Navigating digital financial inclusion in Africa; Releasing private population analytics: How should we do it?; CMU faculty selected for UN-led working group on DPI safeguards; picoCTF-Africa inspires participants to learn cybersecurity; Protecting software secrets in medical systems; A Low-Cost Honeynet Infrastructure For Smishing Data Collection; Towards A Low-Cost Mobile Internet Measurement Infrastructure: An Initial Deployment in Africa; Analysis of the Internet Ecosystem in East Africa: DNS resolution, ASN peering, and Utilization of IXPs.;   A Water Efficiency Dataset for African Data Centers, https://www.climatechange.ai/events/neurips2024, Vancouver, Canada, December 15 2024.; HumekaFL: Automated Detection of Neonatal Asphyxia Using Federated Learning- Pamely Zantou, Blessed Guda, Gladys Inabeza, Bereket Retta, Carlee Joe-Wong and Assane Gueye- ACM Compass Conference India July 8th 2024.

subsections.title: Summary of Year 4 2025 Plan
subsections.overview: DPG/DPI - Develop a course that will introduce students and early career enthusiasts to Digital ID. The team plans to use this course to develop more skills and interests and come up with new use cases for digital ID while driving the digital transformation across Africa. Continue working on using generative AI in the digital ID process to facilitate and streamline cross-border payments. Continue testing vulnerabilities of cited DPGs/DPIs and providing them feedback on how to improve their security systems. Test, develop, and possibly roll out a Digital ID-assisted authentication use case geared towards providing digital ID inclusion for the most vulnerable through wallets ( OpenWallet). Rekindle engagements with Mifos to assess how more use cases can be developed and how the platform's security and usability features can be improved.;     Cybersecurity - Continue sharing security reports with each country willing to receive feedback and assess improvement. In collaboration with CMU-Africa’s Afretec program, the team plans to work with eight African universities to install their cybersecurity labs at scale, starting with the Security Operations Centre. The team plans to scale up the installation of the Honey systems across multiple Universities in Africa.;        Public Health, Agriculture and Climate Change - The Upanzi Network plans to continue improving the platform's security system and engaging health actors to deploy the platform in more Africa-based health centers. Scale up the work into other health facilities and develop a public-facing dashboard to share the data and insights collected from the project. Publish a paper and present at multiple global health forums and conferences. Scale the research to other countries and expand the research questions and parameters to include more climate variables. Present the published paper  at multiple conferences.;         Data - In year 4, continue scaling up the data wrangling on the open data portal, improving the platform's user interface, and adding more datasets. Integrate the data quality framework, transforming the platform into an open data quality assessment platform. This will give the data users more context, usability, and nuisance. Integrate AI to automate the data tagging and description process and the data quality assessments. Keep working on the user interface and improving data integration using Google APIs. Deploy applications in at least one health facility. Start developing a paper that will also be published and presented at multiple conferences.;         Connectivity - Deploy more measurement pods in at least 5 countries. Expand the measurement scope, looking closely at the boomerang data pipelines. Develop and publish a paper on the research findings. Scale up its work with public transport providers p to use buses as data carriers in a delay tolerant mesh network. Deploy the technology in the refugee camp and integrate it with the WASH information sharing system as well as sharing Educaiton content across the the camp. start and scale up the work by installing the system on drones with Dronlystics drone company, which is used to bring internet connectivity to rural areas while spraying agricultural fields.;     Cpacity Building - Scale up the program by engaging more universities and high schools in at least 15 countries, focusing on the new and least engaged countries in the past, including Malawi and Madagascar. Improve the training and curriculum by including some simulations and demonstrations. Scale up the work done during the inaugural Digital ID Hackathon, which focused on students from East Africa to West, North, and Southern Africa. Through the Upanzi partners and nodes, the team plans to work closely with the University of Dakar, the University of Botswana, and Wits University to increase awareness and participation. Engage Anyanworks, the company behind the Credbel Digital ID platform, to increase the number of Digital ID platforms participants can use to develop their ideas. Replicate the internship program, allowing CMU-Africa students to participate in projects implemented within the lab. The team also plans to continue the research projects in collaboration with faculty and staff at CMU-Africa. Continue with and complete the existing projects with Approov, RURA (Rwanda Utilities Regulatory Authority), AfricaCERT, eGov. Through more stakeholder engagements, the team plans to increase the number of externship projects, prioritizing the most aligned projects with organizations that include Mifos and OpenWallet. Starting in November 2024, the team plans to kick off the professional-in-residence program with a team of 3 personnels from the Ethiopia National ID program. They will have a six-month capacity-building engagement to tackle current issues identified while deploying their National Digital ID infrastructure.;     Technology and Society - Dig deeper into this research by focusing on responsible AI and Machine learning. Building upon the completed RRI framework, the team plans to create one for Responsible AI and test it on the AI projects currently available in the lab. The team also plans to assist with projects that need to meet the mark to assess and improve.;     Knowledge Transfer - Implement phase 2 of the DEC implementation by making the center self-paced and self-guided. The team plans to curate and install explainer videos that provide a digitally guided space tour. Invite at least 150 stakeholders to the space to showcase the research and work implemented in the lab. Invite other stakeholders, NGOs, government, academia, and development partners to showcase their research and digital products in the DEC space. Scale up its advisory services to at least five African governments. It will continue engaging with the Ethiopian and Rwandan Governments and seek to support the governments of Senegal, The Gambia, and Morocco on their digital transformation journeys. Attend and present at the Africa Health Tech Summit in Rwanda and the Global Digital Health Network in Kenya. The team plans to collaborate with eGov and CRS to host an eGov DPI health campaign in February 2025. ID4Africa 2025, the Upanzi team plans to attend and present the winners of the Digital ID Hackathons from East, South, West, and Northern Africa winners. The AI for Good summit is an event on the Upanzi team's radar. At the summit, the team plans to share some of the AI work implemented in the lab. Other conference events on the Upanzi Network radar include the Word Data forum, the Rise of AI conference, and the sustainability conference.;       Upanzi Network Expansion - launch the Al Akhawayn University node, starting with the National Cybersecurity Symposium. The node will focus on Morocco's digital transformation agenda, specifically the digital ID ecosystem currently scaling up in Morocco. Through the University of Botswana, the team plans to launch an Upanzi Network node that uses Digital tools to improve livestock management. The team also plans to assess the possibilities of scaling up and raising awareness of the animal biometrics project, which is considered a priority in the country. With Wits University Department of Digital Arts, The Hub for Multilingual Education and Literacies (HuMEL), and the Games, the Upanzi Network will launch a node focusing on using digital tools and technology combined with virtual reality to improve linguistic learning. Continue engaging multiple universities in Ethiopia, assessing the best departments to bring on board as a new Upanzi Network node. The team will continue to solicit the best-aligned institutions and departments by working with the government's National Digital ID program. engage the University of Gaston Berger and invite the information technology department to apply to join the Upanzi Network.
